description: A curated list of 300 top data structures and algorithm questions covering all major patterns.

topics.name: Arrays & Hashing
explanation: Arrays represent a contiguous block of memory, allowing for O(1) time complexity when indexing elements. However, inserting or deleting elements in the middle of an array requires shifting elements, which takes O(N) time.
Hash tables (or Hash Maps) provide incredibly fast O(1) average time complexity for insertions, deletions, and lookups by mapping keys to indices via a hash function.

When combined, arrays and hash maps are frequently used to solve mapping, frequency analysis, and element lookup problems optimally. Always consider hash maps when you need to repeatedly check for the existence of an element.

topics.name: Two Pointers
explanation: The Two Pointers technique typically involves using two indices to iterate through an array or list, often starting at opposite ends or moving at different speeds.
This dynamic allows you to process pairs of elements simultaneously, dramatically reducing nested loops and achieving O(N) time complexity.
This pattern is heavily reliant on ordered/sorted data, or structures where elements have an inherent relationship (e.g., palindromes).

problems.title: Two Sum II - Input Array Is Sorted
Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array II

topics.name: Sliding Window
explanation: Sliding Window is a subset of two pointers used to monitor a contiguous subsequence (subarray or substring) of elements.
We maintain a 'window' bound by two pointers (left and right). The right pointer expands the window to satisfy a condition, while the left pointer shrinks it when the condition is violated.
This turns O(N^2) brute-force nested loops into efficient O(N) single-pass solutions by sliding over the collection rather than recalculating overlapping segments.

problems.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
Number of Substrings Containing All Three Characters
Find All Anagrams in a String
Longest Subarray of 1's After Deleting One Element

topics.name: Stack
explanation: A Stack is a Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) data structure. You can only push items onto the top and pop items off the top.
Stacks naturally track state that needs to be reversed or evaluated backwards, making them perfect for checking matching pairs (like parentheses), undo operations, and evaluating expressions.

A 'Monotonic Stack' is a specialized stack heavily tested in interviews; it strictly maintains either an increasing or decreasing order. That makes it perfect for finding the 'next greater' or 'next smaller' element in O(N) time.

topics.name: Queues
explanation: A Queue is a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) data structure. The first element added to the queue will be the first one to be removed, much like a line at a store.
Queues are the backbone of Breadth-First Search (BFS) and level-order traversals in graphs and trees.
There are variations of queues such as standard Queues, Deques (Double Ended Queues where you can push/pop from both ends), and Priority Queues (heaps).

problems.title: Number of Students Unable to Eat Lunch

topics.name: Binary Search
explanation: Binary Search drastically reduces the search space by half at every step. This makes it possible to search millions of records in mere milliseconds, providing a logarithmic O(log N) time complexity.
While standard binary search finds targets in sorted arrays, competitive problems often challenge you to apply binary search on a 'Search Space'.

If you can determine that a function is monotonically increasing or decreasing (e.g., 'if X works, then X+1 works' or vice versa), you can use binary search to find the optimal answer in a massive range.

problems.title: Find First and Last Position of Element in Sorted Array
Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array II
Search in Rotated Sorted Array II
Capacity To Ship Packages Within D Days
Minimum Number of Days to Make m Bouquets
Kth Smallest Element in a Sorted Matrix
Count of Smaller Numbers After Self

topics.name: Linked List
explanation: Linked Lists consist of nodes sequentially pointing to each other. Unlike arrays, they are not contiguous in memory, preventing O(1) random access but allowing O(1) insertions or deletions if you hold a reference to the node.
Always pay attention to edge cases: Empty lists, single node lists, and operating on the head/tail directly.
To ease list operations and avoid painful edge cases when modifying the head, instantiate a 'Dummy Node' pointing to the head. Return dummy.next as your final answer.

problems.title: Remove Nth Node From End of List
Flatten a Multilevel Doubly Linked List

topics.name: Trees & BST
explanation: Trees represent hierarchical state mappings. A tree must contain a single root node and cannot contain cycles.
You'll traverse trees via Depth First Search (DFS - pre, in, or post order using recursion/stacks) or Breadth First Search (BFS - level order using queues).

A Binary Search Tree (BST) specifically orders data such that all left descendants are smaller than the parent, and all right descendants are larger. An in-order traversal of a BST maps elements in sorted order.

problems.title: Lowest Common Ancestor of a BST
Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Tree
Binary Tree Zigzag Level Order Traversal
Count Good Nodes in Binary Tree
Kth Smallest Element in a BST
Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder Traversal
Construct Binary Tree from Inorder and Postorder Traversal
Convert Sorted Array to Binary Search Tree
Flatten Binary Tree to Linked List
Populating Next Right Pointers in Each Node
Vertical Order Traversal of a Binary Tree

topics.name: Heap / Priority Queue
explanation: A Heap is a complete binary tree that satisfies the heap property: in a max-heap, every parent is larger than its children; in a min-heap, every parent is smaller.
Heaps provide O(log N) push and pop and O(1) peek at the max or min element, making them the go-to structure for 'top K' problems.
The 'Two Heaps' pattern is a powerful technique: maintain a max-heap of the smaller half and a min-heap of the larger half to efficiently track medians or balanced partitions.

problems.title: Kth Largest Element in an Array
Kth Largest Element in a Stream

topics.name: Graphs & Matrices
explanation: A Graph models pairwise connections (edges) between isolated entities (vertices/nodes).

Graphs can be represented through Adjacency Lists (best for sparse graphs) or Matrices (best for dense graphs). Make sure to carefully track visited nodes in a set to avoid spinning into infinite calculation cycles.

Key Graph Algorithms: Breadth-First Search (shortest unweighted paths), Depth-First Search (deep explorations or topological sorts), Dijkstra's Algorithm (shortest weighted paths), and Union Find (disjoint sets bridging networks).

problems.title: Number of Connected Components in Undirected Graph
Min Cost to Connect All Points (Kruskal's)

topics.name: Dynamic Programming
explanation: Dynamic Programming (DP) revolves around breaking a larger, complex problem into simpler, identical, overlapping subproblems.
Instead of redundantly calculating the same subproblem repeatedly, DP stores those computed answers into memory.
You can deploy DP via Memoization (Recursion passing state down into a cache map) or Tabulation (Iterative approach building out a completely formulated array iteratively from the bottom up).

problems.title: Minimum Cost Tree From Leaf Values
Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with Cooldown
Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with Transaction Fee
Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock IV

topics.name: Greedy
explanation: Greedy algorithms make the locally optimal choice at each step, hoping to reach a globally optimal solution. Unlike DP, they don't reconsider past decisions.
Greedy works when a problem has an 'optimal substructure' and a 'greedy choice property' — meaning the locally best choice leads to the globally best answer.
The key to mastering greedy is proving correctness: sort data to impose structure, then make a single-pass decision at each step.

problems.title: Merge Triplets to Form Target Triplet
Minimum Number of Arrows to Burst Balloons

topics.name: Backtracking
explanation: Backtracking is a refined brute-force approach that incrementally builds candidates and abandons them (backtracks) as soon as it determines a candidate cannot lead to a valid solution.
The core loop is: choose an option, recurse into it, and then undo that choice before trying the next option. This 'undo' is the backtrack step.
Always think about how to prune the search tree early to avoid exploring unnecessary branches — this is where sorting before recursing pays off.

problems.title: Letter Combinations of a Phone Number

topics.name: Tries
explanation: A Trie (prefix tree) is a tree-like structure where each node represents a single character. Paths from root to marked nodes spell out valid words.
Tries are optimal for prefix-based searches — finding all words with a given prefix is O(P + K) where P is prefix length and K is results count.
Bit Tries store binary representations of numbers, enabling O(32) XOR queries — useful for maximum XOR problems.

problems.title: Design Add and Search Words Data Structure
Maximum XOR of Two Numbers in an Array
Count Pairs With XOR in a Range

topics.name: Intervals
explanation: Interval problems involve ranges [start, end] and typically require detecting overlaps, merging overlapping ranges, or scheduling non-conflicting intervals.
Almost always, the first step is to sort intervals by start time. Once sorted, a single pass determines overlaps.
Sweep Line is a powerful pattern: process events (interval starts and ends) in sorted order using a counter or heap.

problems.title: Minimum Interval to Include Each Query

topics.name: Bit Manipulation
explanation: Bit manipulation exploits the binary representation of numbers using operators: AND (&), OR (|), XOR (^), NOT (~), left shift (<<), right shift (>>).
XOR is especially powerful: x ^ x = 0 and x ^ 0 = x. This makes it ideal for finding lone elements in arrays where everything else appears twice.
Bit masking enumerates subsets of N elements in O(2^N) using integers 0 to 2^N - 1, where each bit represents whether an element is included.

topics.name: Math & Geometry
explanation: Math-based problems rely on number properties, modular arithmetic, prime factorization, GCD/LCM, or combinatorics formulas rather than complex data structures.
Geometry problems often revolve around coordinate manipulation, slope comparisons, or area calculations.
Simulation problems ask you to model a process step by step — the key is identifying the pattern that avoids an O(N^2) brute simulation.

topics.name: Advanced Graphs
explanation: Advanced graph algorithms solve more specialized problems: MST for minimum cost connectivity, Bellman-Ford for graphs with negative weights, Floyd-Warshall for all-pairs shortest paths.
Bridges and articulation points identify critical edges/nodes whose removal disconnects the graph — solved using DFS timestamps.
Strongly Connected Components (Tarjan's or Kosaraju's) decompose a directed graph into maximal subgraphs where every node is reachable from every other.

problems.title: Min Cost to Connect All Points (Prim's / Kruskal's)
Find Critical and Pseudo-Critical Edges in MST
Find the City With the Smallest Number of Neighbors at a Threshold Distance
Number of Operations to Make Network Connected

topics.name: Segment Tree & Fenwick Tree
explanation: Segment Trees support O(log N) range queries (sum, min, max) and O(log N) point updates, handling dynamic arrays that change over time.
Fenwick Trees (Binary Indexed Trees) are a simpler, more cache-friendly structure for prefix sum queries and point updates in O(log N).
These structures shine when you need repeated range queries on a mutable array — static arrays use prefix sums instead.

problems.title: Count of Smaller Numbers After Self

topics.name: String Algorithms
explanation: String-specific algorithms go beyond simple iteration. KMP and Z-function find patterns in O(N + M) by preprocessing the pattern to skip redundant comparisons.
Rolling Hash (Rabin-Karp) computes a hash in O(1) for any substring, enabling O(N) average-case pattern matching.
Manacher's algorithm finds all palindromic substrings in O(N), far better than the O(N^2) expand-around-center approach.

problems.title: Find the Index of the First Occurrence in a String (KMP)
Check If Two String Arrays are Equivalent

topics.name: Sorting
explanation: Understanding sorting algorithms — their time complexity, space complexity, and stability — is fundamental. Merge Sort is O(N log N) stable; Quick Sort is O(N log N) average but O(N^2) worst case in-place.
Non-comparison sorts like Counting Sort and Radix Sort achieve O(N) by exploiting constraints on the data (e.g., bounded integers).
Often in interviews, sorting the data first is the key insight that unlocks a greedy or two-pointer solution.

problems.title: Kth Largest Element in an Array (QuickSelect)
